oai_dc:dc.dc:title: 2024 Blue-throated Macaw Breeding Season Breaks Records
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Paterson, Dorothy
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: This article originally appeared in AFA Watchbird Volume 51 Number 3 (July 2024). The text below contains excerpts from the original article. For photographs, figures, advertisements, and the complete formatted version as originally published, please use the PDF link associated with this article. Great news! A record-breaking 17 Blue-throated Macaws successfully fledged from the Nido Adoptivo nest boxes on the Laney Rickman Reserve in Bolivia this year, bringing the total to an amazing 145 Blue-throated Macaw chicks that have fledged into the wild since the project began. The nest box project was "hatched" in 2006 by my late sister Laney Rickman, founder of Bird Endowment, and Bennett Hennessey of Asociacion Armonia during the Loro Parque International Parrot Convention in Tenerife, Spain. Their shared commitment to "Saving the Blues" led to the formation of our wonderful, long-term partnership which laid the solid foundation for the project's success over the years. When the project began, the population of the Blue-throated Macaw was alarmingly low, with only 100 to 150 individuals remaining - and declining, primarily due to habitat degradation causing a lack of suitable nesting sites. By 2015, Armonia's population survey estimated numbers had grown to 420 to 480 Blue-throated Macaws. The 145 chicks that have fledged from the nest boxes make up almost half of that population increase and about one-third of the total estimated population. The nest box project has truly made a significant impact. The shift in IUCN designation from "Critically Endangered - declining" to "Critically Endangered - stable" is a great indication that we are on the right path to saving this exquisite bird from extinction. The steepening in recent years of the curve in the graph of the cumulative number of Blue-throated Macaw fledges is very encouraging. This steepening indicates that conservation strategies are effectively addressing the threats faced by the Blue-throated Macaw. After a slow buildup over 16 years, the project celebrated the significant milestone of the 100th chick fledged in 2021. Extrapolation of the trendline on the graph suggests there is a possibility of reaching the next milestone of the 200th chick in 2027. How incredible that this span between the 100th and the 200th chicks could be only six years! This trend indicates a significant acceleration in the number of chicks successfully fledging annually, a crucial indicator of population recovery and conservation success. Click the PDF link associated with this article to view the complete article as originally published in AFA Watchbird.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: AFA Watchbird; Vol. 51 No. 3 (2024): AFA Watchbird July 2024
